rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF FRANCES JOSEPHINE THOMPSON
rulings.Calendar Matter: Verified Petition To Determine Heirship And Entitlement To Estate
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 7/27/22. No earlier available date. Decedent's birth certificate does not identify HER by name and no supplemental report to the Certificate of Birth was attached as exhibit 46. Rose Belmont Thompson's death certificate shows her father as James Ritchie, not John Lincoln Ritchie, and she was not named in her sister Alice Elizabeth Richie Morrow's obituary, although she survived her sister? If John Michael Ritchie was survived by his wife, who also post-deceased the decedent, who is her successor-in-interest? Notice of the 5/18/22 hearing was mailed to Ann L. Ritchie on 5/9/22, which was not at least 15 days' notice. Petitioner alleges in paragraph 23 of the amended petition that there are no paternal heirs. Who are the other people, many with the last name Thompson, who were identified in the initial Petition for Letters of Administration and who received notice? (The error in Patricia Louise Ritchie Ripley's marriage certificate has not yet been cleared up.) Courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues must be received by the assigned Examiner by no later than 7/18/22. (A supplement or amendment to a petition should stand on its own and not "answer" issues raised by an examiner.) Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: THE AVINOAM MANDELBROT FAMILY TRUST DATED JULY 19, 2005
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Expunge Notices Of Pendency Of Action Alternatively To Require Petitioners To File Undertaking Alternatively For Trustee To Post Undertaking
rulings.Rulings: Part 1 of 4 This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Respondent, Tzipora Sobol as successor trustee of the Avinoam Mandelbrot Family Trust, has brought this motion to expunge notices of pendency of action that Petitioners recorded on the following pieces of real property: 1155 EVANS DRIVE, SANTA ROSA, CA; 510-512 TODD ROAD, SANTA ROSA, CA; 7488 BRIDGIT DRIVE, ROHNERT PARK, CA; 2640-2644-2650 MISSION STREET, SAN FRANCISCO, CA; 2533 MORAGA STREET, SAN FRANCISCO, CA; 1696 VALENCIA STREET, SAN FRANCISO, CA. In the alternative, Respondent asks the court to require an undertaking either in lieu of or in addition to the notices of lis pendens. Respondent's Request for Judicial Notice is GRANTED. Notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660.) Petitioners' Evidentiary Objections are OVERULED. Respondent's motion to expunge the notices of lis pendens recorded on the properties listed above pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 405.31 is DENIED. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 405.20, "A party to an action who asserts a real property claim may record a notice of pendency of action in which that real property claim is alleged." As stated in J&A Mash & Barrel, LLC v. Sup. Ct. of Fresno Cnty. (2022) 74 Cal. App. 5th 1, 22, "The only substantive grounds upon which a lis pendens can be expunged is if the underlying action does not contain a real property claim [(Code of Civil Procedure section 405.31)] or the real property claim lacks probable validity [(Code of Civil Procedure section 405.32)]." Per the Notice of Motion, this motion to expunge is brought only under Code of Civil Procedure section 405.31. On a motion to expunge a lis pendens, the burden of proof is on the party opposing the motion to establish the existence of a "real property claim." (Code of Civil Procedure section 405.30). The statute defines "real property claim" to be "the cause or causes of action in a pleading which would, if meritorious, affect (a) title to, or the right to possession of, specific real property or (b) the use of an easement identified in the pleading, other than an easement obtained pursuant to statute by any regulated public utility." (Code of Civil Procedure section 405.4) A "real property claim" requires a pleading to (1) identify specific real property that is the subject of the action, and (2) show that a cause or causes of action would affect title to or right to possession of the specific property. (Gale v. Sup.Ct. (Gale) (2004) 122 CA4th 1388, 1396) Kirkeby v. Sup. Ct. (2004) 33 Cal. 4th 642, 647-48, discusses the "demurrer-like analysis" the trial court applies to the pleading when reviewing a motion to expunge under Section 405.31. Section 405.31 provides: "In proceedings under this chapter, the court shall order the notice expunged if the court finds that the pleading on which the notice is based does not contain a real property claim." In making this determination, the court must engage in a demurrer-like analysis. "Rather than analyzing whether the pleading states any claim at all, as on a general demurrer, the court must undertake the more limited analysis of whether the pleading states a real property claim." [citation.] Review "involves only a review of the adequacy of the pleading and normally should not involve evidence from either side, other than possibly that which may be judicially noticed as on a demurrer." [citation.] Therefore, review of an expungement order under section 405.31 is limited to whether a real property claim has been properly pled by the claimant. [citation.] 33 Cal. 4th 642, 647-48. Part 1 of 4

rulings.Case Title: THE AVINOAM MANDELBROT FAMILY TRUST DATED JULY 19, 2005
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Expunge Notices Of Pendency Of Action Alternatively To Require Petitioners To File Undertaking Alternatively For Trustee To Post Undertaking (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Part 2 of 4 Having undertaken a "demurrer-like" review of the Petition Concerning Internal Affairs of the Trust and for Elder Financial Abuse, the Court finds that the pleading contains a "real property claim." The Petition identifies specific real property that is the subject of the action. It lists the specific real property at issue in the litigation at Paragraph 37, by the properties' street addresses. This is sufficient identification of the real property at issue in this litigation to allow a search of public records by interested parties. The Petition contains causes of action would affect title to or right to possession of the specific property listed if the Petitioners prevail on their claims. Specifically, if the Petitioners' claims are successful, the 2019 amended Trust will be invalidated and the 2008 amended Trust will be confirmed and in operation. Respondent would be removed as successor trustee; Petitioner Daniel would become successor trustee under the 2008 instrument. Title and control of Trust assets (including the specified real property) would effectively transfer from Respondent to Petitioner Daniel. Further, all four of the Petitioners would become beneficiaries under the 2008 instrument with individual interests in the Trust assets; under the 2019 instrument, they are disinherited and have no interest in the Trust assets. Respondent's request in the alternative that Respondent be required to post a de minimus undertaking pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 405.33 in lieu of a notice of lis pendens is DENIED. A proper lis pendens may be expunged if the court finds "adequate relief can be secured to the claimant by the giving of an undertaking." (Code of Civil Procedure section 405.33). The purpose of the undertaking or bond is to allow "the defendant to dispose of the property subject to suit while remaining capable of responding in damages to the plaintiff's claim." (On v. Cow Hollow Properties (1990) 222 CA3d 1568, 1573 (decided under former law).) It is Respondent's burden of proof, as the moving party, to show that an undertaking would provide "adequate relief" to Petitioners if they prevail. (Code Comment to Code of Civil Procedure section 405.30.) "The expungement order shall be conditioned upon the giving of the undertaking of such nature and in such amount as will indemnify the claimant for all damages proximately resulting from the expungement which the claimant may incur if the claimant prevails upon the real property claim." (Code of Civil Procedure section 405.33). Only damages incurred by the claimant and "resulting from the expungement" need be considered when calculating "adequate relief." The value of the land itself and the claimed interest are only factors in determining what damages would result from the expungement. Expungement will not be ordered unless an undertaking will secure "adequate relief" to the claimant, i.e., here, the Petitioners (Code of Civil Procedure section 405.33). The motion's suggested relief in the alternative seems to reverse this protective purpose by requesting that Respondent be bonded for little or nothing. Respondent has not met the burden to show that the requested undertaking would provide "adequate relief" to Petitioners, and "indemnify the [Petitioners] for all damages proximately resulting from the expungement which the [Petitioners] may incur if [they prevail] upon the real property claim" (Code of Civil Procedure section 405.33). Respondent's request in the alternative that Petitioners be required to post a $2.3 million dollar undertaking as a condition of maintaining the lis pendens pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 405.34 is DENIED. Part 2 of 4

rulings.Case Title: THE AVINOAM MANDELBROT FAMILY TRUST DATED JULY 19, 2005

rulings.Case Title: THE AVINOAM MANDELBROT FAMILY TRUST DATED JULY 19, 2005
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Expunge Notices Of Pendency Of Action Alternatively To Require Petitioners To File Undertaking Alternatively For Trustee To Post Undertaking (Added For The Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Part 3 of 4 Per the language of Section 405.34, any interested person may seek this relief, and it does not matter whether a motion to expunge is pending. "An undertaking required pursuant to this section shall be of such nature and in such amount as the court may determine to be just." Recovery under the undertaking requires a showing that claimant did not prevail, and that the requesting party suffered damages as a result of the lis pendens being maintained. Again, Respondent has the burden of proof. Respondent, via the Donner declaration, offers projected carrying costs for the next two years on the Mission Street commercial property ($283, 340, at par. 5B); the Moraga single-family home ($88,600 at par. 5B); and the Sonoma properties collectively ($227,000, at par. 10C). These amounts total $598,940. There are no exhibits or other evidence submitted to support these numbers, however. The remainder of the requested $2.3 million dollar bond appears to be based on Donner's speculation that there will be significant losses if the sales on the Mission Street and Moraga Street properties specifically, and the Sonoma properties generally, are not allowed to proceed (see Donner Decl. par. 5A, 8B, 8C, 10A, 10B, 10C, 11). Respondent has not presented sufficient evidence to carry the burden of proof here. On the current record, the court cannot determine whether "An undertaking required pursuant to this section [405.34] [would] be of such nature and in such amount as [] to be just." Respondent's request for attorney fees and costs pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 405.38 is DENIED. Petitioners' request for attorney fees and costs pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 405.38 is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 405.38, "The court shall direct that the party prevailing on any motion under this chapter be awarded the reasonable attorney's fees and costs of making or opposing the motion unless the court finds that the other party acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of attorney's fees and costs unjust." Here, the Petitioners are the "prevailing party" for purposes of section 405.38, insofar as they "realized [their] litigation objectives by successfully defending against the motion to expunge the lis pendens, thereby maintaining protections to [the] real property claim until the matter is fully adjudicated at trial." (J&A Mash & Barrel, LLC v. Sup. Ct. (2022) 74 Cal. App. 5th 1, 42-43.) Petitioners seek $4,725 in costs and $47,467 in attorneys' fees in connection with the motion to expunge and the related ex parte petition to advance the hearing on the motion. (Christo Decl. par. 19 and 20). Respondent offers no specific response to the requests, other than to characterize them as "grotesque" and argue that she should be awarded fees, not Petitioners. (Reply Br., p. 9.) The court finds Petitioners are the party prevailing on the motion and awards $20,000 in attorneys' fees and costs to Petitioners pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 405.38. The court believes that the fees and costs Petitioners have requested exceed what would be considered "reasonable attorney's fees and costs of [ ] opposing the motion" under the statute, and based on the record before the court. Respondent is to pay Petitioners $20,000 within 10 days of notice of entry of this order. Petitioners are ordered to lodge with the clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling, as published above, by the time set for this hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the court day before the hearing. Part 3 of 4

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE HAROLD K. LIPSET TRUST F/B/O LAWRENCE J. LIPSET
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition To Modify Trust Due To Changed Circumstances
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 07/27/2022 by the assigned judicial officer after review. The following issues must be addressed: 1) Did the settlor anticipate that the size of Louis Lipset's estate might exceed $600,000 when the settlor signed the first amendment in 1996? i) If yes, why did the settlor provide for distribution of one-half share of the residue as well as the balance of Lawrence Lipset's share after his death to Louis Lipset outright and free of trust? ii) If no, should the increase of Louis Lipset' wealth be a changed circumstance that need be alleged as a basis of modification under section 15409? 2) Does the petitioner intend to request the proposed modification under section 15403? If so, the petition must be amended. A guardian ad litem may be required for minor and unascertained beneficiaries. See section 15405. PARTIES APPEARING SHOULD CHECK IN FOR THE HEARING WITH THEIR FULL NAME. Counsel and parties may appear personally or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. For a video appearance, go to zoom.us, click "join a meeting" and input meeting ID 875 8017 7925 and password 722809. For an audio-only appearance through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and key in meeting ID "875 8017 7925#", then participant ID "0#", then password "722809#". (Toll rates may apply).) The Court prefers that counsel and parties appearing by video input their first and last name into the "Your Name" dialogue box. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling 1-888-882-6878. Counsel or parties appearing in person must wear a mask covering their nose and mouth at all times while in the courthouse. Please review the Court's 11th Amended General Order which took effect on 04/25/2022 and LRSF 14.1(D) for more information.
